anvaya.meaning: whose life is full of sins

translations.verbatim_quote: My dear Arjuna, one who does not follow in human life the cycle of sacrifice thus established by the Vedas certainly leads a life full of sin. Living only for the satisfaction of the senses, such a person lives in vain.
(See holy-bhagavad-gita.org for full Mukundananda rendering; agreement with Prabhupada noted in disagreements section where it diverges materially.)

commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: The mammonist philosophy of ‚Äúwork very hard and enjoy sense gratification‚Äù is condemned herein by the Lord. Therefore, for those who want to enjoy this material world, the above-mentioned cycle of performing yaj√±as is absolutely necessary.

commentaries.translator: Swami Gambhirananda (Advaita Ashrama edition of Sankara-bhasya, as reproduced on bhagavad-gita.us)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: 3.16 O Partha, sah, he; jivati, lives; mogham, in vain; yah, who, though competent for action; na anuvartayati, does not follow; iha, here, in the world; cakram, the wheel of the world; evam, thus; pravartitam, set in motion, by God, on the basis of the Vedas and the sacrifices; a
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Shankara reads cakra·πÅ pravartitam as the wheel set in motion by PrajƒÅpati at creation (3.10). The verse is therefore not an exhortation but a diagnostic: one who does not turn the wheel where the wheel was established to be turned (iha) has misaligned with the structure of created order. The three terms (aghƒÅyu·∏•, indriyƒÅrƒÅma·∏•, mogham jƒ´vati) name the three failures: a life accumulates sin (aghƒÅyu·∏•), is exhausted in sense-objects rather than yaj√±a (indriyƒÅrƒÅma·∏•), and produces nothing of value within the wheel (mogham ‚Äî in vain).

commentaries.translator: Swami Adidevananda (Sri Ramakrishna Math edition of Ramanuja's Gita-bhasya, as reproduced on bhagavad-gita.us)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: 3.16 Thus this wheel is set in motion by the Supreme Person: From food arise embodied selves which are denoted by the word ‚Äòbeings‚Äô: from rain food; from sacrifice rain; sacrifice from activities which constitute the exercise of an agent; and activity from the embodied self; and a
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Ramanuja reads the verse in the context of the chain instituted by the Lord (3.14-3.15): yaj√±a sustains the devas, devas send rains, rains produce food, food produces creatures, creatures perform yaj√±a. The one who does not turn this chain forward ‚Äî by performing acts as worship of the Lord rather than for sense-indulgence ‚Äî is the thief named at 3.12. 'Lives in vain' (mogham jƒ´vati) means the life is structurally disconnected from its purpose (worship of the Lord through the chain), not merely subjectively empty.

disagreements_among_translators.word: cakra·πÅ pravartitam (the wheel set in motion)

disagreements_among_translators.explanation: Translators agree on the surface verdict (one who does not follow the cycle lives in vain) but emphasise different anchors. Shankara grounds the claim cosmologically ‚Äî the wheel was set in motion by PrajƒÅpati and is the structure of created order itself; not turning it is misalignment with the order. Ramanuja anchors it theologically ‚Äî the wheel is the Lord's institution, and the thief is the one who consumes the wheel's output (devas ‚Üí rains ‚Üí food ‚Üí creatures) without offering back. The verse names three failures stacked: aghƒÅyu·∏• (life-of-sin), indriyƒÅrƒÅma·∏• (sense-indulgent), mogha·πÅ jƒ´vati (lives in vain). Translators flatten these to a single condemnation; the commentaries keep them as three distinct verdicts.

literal_meaning: One who, here in this world, does not follow the wheel of sacrifice thus set in motion lives a life of sin, sense-indulgent, and lives in vain, O PƒÅrtha.
traditional_meaning_consensus: Krishna closes the yaj√±a-cycle argument with a sharp diagnostic: the wheel established by PrajƒÅpati (3.10) and elaborated as anna‚Üíbh≈´ta‚Üív·πõ·π£·π≠i‚Üíyaj√±a‚Üíkarma‚Üíbrahman (3.14-3.15) is not optional. To consume from the wheel without contributing back is to live aghƒÅyu·∏• ‚Äî a life-of-sin ‚Äî because one is a stena (thief, per 3.12). 'Lives in vain' (mogha·πÅ jƒ´vati) is a structural verdict, not an emotional one: the life is not connected to the sustaining cycle, hence it produces nothing the cycle accumulates as merit.
